title: SemEval-2020 Task 7: Assessing Humor in Edited News Headlines
research_questions: Can systems estimate the mean funniness of a micro-edited news headline on a 0-3 humor scale?
Can systems determine which of two edited versions of the same original headline is funnier?
How do participating systems perform across funniness ranges, funniness gaps, and incongruity-driven cases in edited headline humor?
tasks: funniness regression for edited news headlines

datasets_used.name: Humicroedit + FunLines (SemEval-2020 Task 7)
datasets_used.size: 15,095 edited headlines; additional 8,248 annotated FunLines headlines

datasets_used.name: Humicroedit + FunLines (SemEval-2020 Task 7)
datasets_used.size: Subtask 1 train: 9,653; FunLines (Train): 8,248. Subtask 2 train: 9,382; FunLines (Train): 1,959

datasets_used.name: Humicroedit + FunLines (SemEval-2020 Task 7)
datasets_used.size: Subtask 1 dev: 2,420, test: 3,025. Subtask 2 dev: 2,356, test: 2,961

methods_proposed.name: SemEval-2020 Task 7 benchmark
methods_proposed.summary: The paper defines a shared benchmark for assessing humor caused by single-word micro-edits in news headlines, with Subtask 1 as 0-3 funniness regression and Subtask 2 as pairwise funnier-headline classification.

methods_proposed.name: Reward metric for Subtask 2
methods_proposed.summary: In addition to accuracy, the paper reports a reward metric that gives larger rewards or penalties when the funniness gap between the two edited headlines is larger.

methods_proposed.name: GloVe-distance incongruity analysis
methods_proposed.summary: For Subtask 2 analysis, the paper operationalizes incongruity as the cosine-distance difference between the original word and each replacement word using GloVe vectors, then correlates that difference with the ground-truth pair label.

humor_theories.detail: Section 6.2.1 tests incongruity theory by assuming larger GloVe cosine distance between the original word and replacement word indicates higher expected incongruity.
The discussion of example R3 says the humorous effect involves tension relief, which is described as complex to model.

evaluation_methods.detail: Qualified Amazon Mechanical Turk workers edited headlines, and five judges rated each edited headline on a 0-3 scale; the mean of the five ratings is the funniness score.
Subtask 1 systems are ranked by RMSE against the mean human funniness rating.
Subtask 2 systems are ranked by classification accuracy; the paper also reports a reward metric weighted by the absolute funniness difference between the two edited headlines.

headline_results.finding: Hitachi was the best system for Subtask 1.
Hitachi was the best system for Subtask 2.
headline_results.value: 67.43%; 0.2988 (a 17.93 increase in percentage points over BASELINE)

headline_results.finding: RoBERTa was the best benchmark for Subtask 1 in the official results table.
RoBERTa was the best benchmark for Subtask 2 in the official results table.
The shared task was the largest shared humor task to date in terms of participation.
The top 20 systems for Subtask 2 were all well above the baseline.

key_findings: The task attracted more than 300 signups, 86 development-phase teams, and 48 and 31 evaluation-phase teams for the two subtasks.
Pre-trained language models performed strongly, and the top systems used ensembles of models such as BERT, RoBERTa, GPT-2, XLNet, Transformer-XL and XLM.
Context-sensitive BERT and RoBERTa embeddings outperformed the context-independent GloVe CBOW benchmark in Table 3.
For Subtask 1, systems had their minimum error at a funniness score of about 1.0 and larger errors toward the extremes of the funniness scale.
For Subtask 2, larger ground-truth funniness gaps made ranking easier, and the paper finds that incongruity was positively correlated with more accurate humor ranking.
The paper identifies world knowledge, cultural references, sarcasm, negative sentiment, and common-sense reasoning as recurring sources of system errors.
limitations: Extreme funniness scores were less frequent in the dataset, and systems had larger errors toward the extremes of the funniness scale.
Some headlines showed lack of sufficient agreement between judges; the paper notes that more judges could improve reliability but would increase cost.
Some teams reported dataset bias around frequent mentions of President Trump and headlines mentioning both “Trump” and “hair” receiving high humor scores.
FunLines was annotated by a different set of judges, so its rating scale may not be calibrated with the main task dataset.
Participating systems struggled with world knowledge, cultural references, sarcasm, negative sentiment, tension relief, and common-sense reasoning.
Most Subtask 2 systems reused Subtask 1 regression systems, and the authors say future versions should make Subtask 2 more independent.
safety_ethics_notes: No explicit safety or ethics section is provided; the paper notes dataset bias involving Trump-related headlines and discusses subjectivity and judge disagreement in humor ratings.

artifacts.other: Competition page: https://competitions.codalab.org/competitions/20970; FunLines dataset: https://cs.rochester.edu/u/nhossain/funlines.html; FunLines game website: https://funlines.co
